title: Heaps / Priority Queue
summary: A heap is an array that pretends to be a balanced tree so you can grab the smallest (or largest) element in $O(\log n)$ — in C++ that's `std::priority_queue`. You've never used heaps, but four patterns cover ~90% of interview heap questions: **top-K**, **K-way merge**, **two-heaps running median**, and **greedy scheduling** — and Flipkart-style hards lean on them explicitly.

notes.heading: Why heaps exist — and when to grab one
notes.body:
Sorting gives you the *full* order of $n$ elements for $O(n \log n)$. But a huge family of problems never needs the full order — they only ever ask **"what's the current extreme?"**: the largest, the smallest, the most frequent, the most urgent. Paying for a full sort every time something changes is the naive trap. A **heap** (priority queue) is the data structure that answers exactly that one question, fast, even while elements keep arriving:

- `push(x)` — $O(\log n)$
- `pop()` the extreme element — $O(\log n)$
- `top()` peek at the extreme — $O(1)$

> [!ANALOGY]
> A hospital ER triage queue. Patients arrive in any order; the desk always knows *who is most critical right now* without re-sorting the whole waiting room after every arrival. Nobody can ask "who's 14th in line?" — the queue only ever reveals the front. That's a heap: instant access to the extreme, nothing else.

Under the hood it's just an array with a mental tree drawn on top: element $i$'s children live at $2i+1$ and $2i+2$, its parent at $(i-1)/2$. The only rule ("heap property") is *parent beats children*. That's much weaker than sorted order — which is exactly why maintaining it is cheap.

[[fig:m08-array-tree]]

Reach for a heap when you see any of these:

- **"K-th largest / smallest", "top K", "K most frequent"** — and you don't need the items fully sorted.
- You repeatedly **pull the current min/max while new elements keep arriving** (streaming, scheduling, simulation).
- You're **merging several already-sorted lists** and want the next-smallest across all of them.
- You need a **running median** (or other order statistic) over a growing stream.
- A **greedy** algorithm keeps asking "what's the largest / most-urgent thing available *right now*?" — the heap answers in $O(\log n)$ instead of $O(n)$.

> [!KEY]
> A heap does **not** let you search, index, or iterate in order. If you catch yourself wanting to look at the *middle* of the structure, a heap is the wrong tool. It answers exactly one question — "what's the extreme?" — and that's its superpower, not a limitation.

notes.heading: The C++ incantations you must memorize
Template 1 — Top-K largest with a size-K MIN-heap
Template 2 — K-way merge: a heap of next pointers
Template 3 — Two heaps, one median
notes.body:
The running-median problem: numbers arrive one at a time, and after each you must report the median. The trick is realizing you never need the full sorted order — **only the boundary between the smaller half and the larger half**. So maintain exactly that boundary with two heaps facing each other:

- `lo` — a **max-heap** holding the smaller half; its top is the largest small number.
- `hi` — a **min-heap** holding the larger half; its top is the smallest large number.

[[fig:m08-two-heaps]]

Two invariants make the median fall out for free: **ordering** ($\max(lo) \le \min(hi)$) and **balance** ($|lo| - |hi| \in \{0, 1\}$). Then the median is `lo.top()` for an odd count, or the average of the two tops for even.

```cpp
priority_queue<int> lo;                                     // max-heap: smaller half
priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> hi;         // min-heap: larger half

void addNum(int num) {
    lo.push(num);                 // 1) tentatively add to lower half
    hi.push(lo.top()); lo.pop();  // 2) move its max up to keep lo <= hi ordered
    if (hi.size() > lo.size()) {  // 3) rebalance so lo has >= as many as hi
        lo.push(hi.top()); hi.pop();
    }
}
double findMedian() {
    return lo.size() > hi.size() ? lo.top() : (lo.top() + hi.top()) / 2.0;
}
```

Step 2 is the elegant part: instead of *deciding* which half `num` belongs to (with fiddly empty-heap edge cases), you launder it through — push into `lo`, then shove `lo`'s maximum across to `hi`. Whatever crosses over is exactly the element that belongs on the boundary, so the ordering invariant repairs itself. Step 3 only fixes sizes, never ordering.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Insert **5**: `lo={5}` → launder → `hi={5}`, `lo={}` → rebalance → `lo={5}`, `hi={}`. Median **5**.
> Insert **2**: `lo={5,2}`, top 5 crosses → `lo={2}`, `hi={5}`. Sizes equal. Median $(2+5)/2 = \textbf{3.5}$.
> Insert **8**: `lo={8,2}`, top 8 crosses → `hi={5,8}`; now `hi` is bigger, so 5 comes back → `lo={5,2}`, `hi={8}`. Median `lo.top()` = **5**. ✓ (sorted: 2, 5, 8)

> [!PITFALL]
> The classic median bug is skipping the launder (step 2) and writing `if (num < lo.top()) lo.push(num); else hi.push(num);` — which is undefined behavior on the very first insert (`lo` is empty) and easy to get wrong on ties. The three-line launder-then-rebalance has *zero* case analysis. Use it verbatim.

notes.heading: Pitfalls, complexity narration, and when NOT to heap
notes.body:
The recurring bugs and the judgment calls, collected:

- **Default is a MAX-heap** — repeat it until it's boring. Min-heap = `priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>>`.
- **Top-K uses the *opposite* heap.** K largest → size-K **min**-heap. K smallest → size-K **max**-heap. Getting this backwards silently returns wrong answers on large inputs. Verbalize: "min-heap of size k, so the weakest survivor is on top and cheap to kick out."
- **Guard `top()` / `pop()` on empty.** On an empty `priority_queue` they are **undefined behavior**, not an exception — no crash message, just garbage. Always `while (!pq.empty())` or check size first.
- **Two-heaps rebalancing is an invariant, not an afterthought.** After *every* insert, re-establish both $\max(lo) \le \min(hi)$ and the size rule.
- **Ties and stability.** Heaps are not stable. If ties must break by a secondary key, encode it in the comparator (a pair of `(primary, secondary)`) — never hope.

> [!PITFALL] When NOT to use a heap
> If you need the full sorted order anyway — just sort. If $k$ is close to $n$ — sorting is simpler at the same asymptotics. If values live in a small fixed range — counting/bucket sort beats the heap outright (Top K Frequent has an $O(n)$ bucket solution; a median over values in $[0,100]$ needs only 101 counters). Mentioning the non-heap alternative is what separates a good answer from a great one.

> [!EXAM]
> **Name the pattern before coding.** When you spot "K-th", "top K", "median of a stream", or "next-smallest across sorted sources", say it: "this is a two-heaps problem" / "this is a K-way merge." Then lead the complexity narration: "the heap gives me $O(\log k)$ per operation, so $O(n \log k)$ total — versus $O(n \log n)$ for a full sort and $O(n)$ average for quickselect." Interviewers score the framing as much as the code.

problems.name: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
problems.note: Size-K min-heap kept alive as a running structure — builds the 'min-heap for largest' reflex.

problems.name: Last Stone Weight
problems.note: Max-heap simulation: pop the two heaviest, push back the difference if nonzero.

problems.name: Kth Largest Element in an Array
problems.note: The canonical top-K: heap in $O(n \log k)$ — then be ready to contrast with quickselect.

problems.name: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.note: Hash-map count, then heap by frequency; know the $O(n)$ bucket-sort alternative as the follow-up.

problems.name: K Closest Points to Origin
problems.note: Top-K with a custom distance comparator — K *smallest* distances means a size-K **max**-heap.

problems.name: Sort Characters By Frequency
problems.note: Count, heap-order by frequency, rebuild the string from most frequent down.

problems.name: Task Scheduler
problems.note: Greedy-with-heap scheduling: always run the most-frequent still-available task — see the deep dive for the last-window gotcha.

problems.name: Reorganize String
problems.note: Greedy heap: repeatedly place the most-frequent char that isn't the one you just placed.

problems.name: Meeting Rooms II
problems.note: Sort by start, min-heap of end times — the heap's peak size *is* the number of rooms.

problems.name: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
problems.note: K-way merge over rows: heap of (value, r, c), push the successor from the same row.

problems.name: Merge k Sorted Lists
problems.note: The canonical K-way merge — heap of live list heads, never more than one node per list.

problems.name: Find Median from Data Stream
problems.note: The full two-heaps pattern — nail the launder-then-rebalance insert from Template 3.

problems.name: Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums
problems.note: K-way merge dressed as pair-sums: seed the first column of pairs, push neighbors lazily.

problems.name: IPO
problems.note: Two-heap greedy: a capital-sorted unlock stream feeds a max-heap of profits — grab the richest reachable project k times.

deepdives.q: **Find Median from Data Stream** — numbers arrive one at a time; after each arrival you must be able to report the current median essentially instantly. Design the structure.
**Task Scheduler** — given tasks (letters) and a cooldown `n`, two copies of the same task must be at least `n` slots apart. Minimize the total time, counting forced idle slots.
**Merge k Sorted Lists** — you're given k linked lists, each already sorted ascending. Merge them into one sorted list.
**IPO** — you may complete at most `k` projects; each needs a minimum capital to start and pays a profit that adds to your capital. Starting from capital `W`, maximize your final capital.

quiz.q: An endless stream of sensor readings arrives, and after **every** reading you must report the current median. Which approach gives $O(\log n)$ per insert and $O(1)$ per query?
options: Keep a sorted `vector` and insert each reading with binary search
Keep one min-heap of everything and peek at `top()`
A max-heap for the lower half + a min-heap for the upper half, kept balanced
It can't be done with heaps — you need a balanced BST
quiz.explanation: Two heaps maintain exactly the boundary the median lives on: $O(\log n)$ insert, $O(1)$ median from the tops. The sorted vector finds the *position* in $O(\log n)$ but shifting elements to insert costs $O(n)$. A single min-heap only exposes the global minimum — the median is buried in the middle, which heaps can't reach. A BST works but is strictly more code for no gain here.

quiz.q: In C++ you write `priority_queue<int> pq; pq.push(3); pq.push(7); pq.push(5);` — what does `pq.top()` return?
options: Undefined — you must supply a comparator
quiz.explanation: `priority_queue` is a **max-heap by default** (it uses `less<int>`), so `top()` is the largest element: 7. If you wanted 3 (the minimum) you'd need the full incantation `priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>>`. Forgetting this compiles fine and silently gives wrong answers — it's the single most common C++ heap bug.

quiz.q: You need the **20 smallest** values out of $10^6$ elements in $O(n \log k)$ time and $O(k)$ space. Which structure?
options: A size-20 min-heap; pop whenever it exceeds 20
A size-20 max-heap; pop whenever it exceeds 20
A min-heap of all $10^6$ elements; pop 20 times
A max-heap of all $10^6$ elements; pop 20 times
quiz.explanation: Top-K uses the **opposite** heap: K smallest → size-K **max**-heap, so the *largest of your current keepers* sits at `top()` and is cheap to evict when something smaller arrives. Option A keeps evicting the smallest — you'd end up with the 20 *largest*. Option C gives the right answer but costs $O(n)$ space and $O(n \log n)$-ish build time, violating the stated bounds.

quiz.q: You merge $k$ sorted lists containing $N$ nodes total using a min-heap of current heads. What is the time complexity?
options: $O(N \log N)$
$O(N \cdot k)$
$O(k \log N)$
$O(N \log k)$
quiz.explanation: Every one of the $N$ nodes is pushed and popped exactly once, and the heap never holds more than one live head per list — size ≤ $k$ — so each operation is $O(\log k)$: total $O(N \log k)$. $O(N \log N)$ is what concatenate-and-sort costs, i.e. what you're beating. $O(N \cdot k)$ is the no-heap approach of scanning all k heads per output element.

quiz.q: In the two-heaps median, a student replaces the launder step with: `if (num < lo.top()) lo.push(num); else hi.push(num);` followed by size rebalancing. What's the most immediate problem?
options: Undefined behavior on the very first insert — `lo.top()` is called on an empty heap
Nothing — it's exactly equivalent to the launder version
The median becomes an average even for odd counts
Each insert degrades to $O(n)$
quiz.explanation: `top()` on an empty `priority_queue` is undefined behavior — no exception, just garbage or a crash — and `lo` is empty on the first call. Even after patching that with a special case, this style needs careful tie/boundary analysis. The launder trick (`lo.push(num); hi.push(lo.top()); lo.pop();` then rebalance) has zero case analysis, which is exactly why the template uses it. Complexity is unaffected either way, so D is wrong.

quiz.q: You declare `auto cmp = [](int a, int b){ return a < b; };` and build `priority_queue<int, vector<int>, decltype(cmp)> pq(cmp);`. What have you built?
options: A min-heap — `<` means smaller wins
A compile error — lambdas can't be heap comparators
A max-heap — identical to the default
A queue that preserves insertion order
quiz.explanation: The heap comparator returning `true` means "`a` has *lower* priority than `b`" — `a` sinks. With `a < b`, smaller elements sink, so the **largest** floats to `top()`: a max-heap, exactly what the default `less<int>` gives. This inversion trips everyone; for a min-heap you want `>` (or `greater<int>`). Lambdas are fine as comparators as long as you pass the instance to the constructor.

quiz.q: Trace `topKLargest(nums, k)` from Template 1 with `nums = [4, 1, 7, 3, 9]`, `k = 2`. What does the min-heap contain at the end, and what's on top?
options: {4, 7} with 4 on top
{7, 9} with 7 on top
{7, 9} with 9 on top
{9, 4} with 9 on top
quiz.explanation: Push 4 → {4}. Push 1 → {1,4}. Push 7 → size 3 > 2, pop the min (1) → {4,7}. Push 3 → pop 3 → {4,7}. Push 9 → pop 4 → {7,9}. The survivors are the 2 largest, and since it's a *min*-heap, the smaller survivor (7 — the "weakest champion") sits on top, ready to be challenged. If 9 were on top you'd have a max-heap, which would evict your best elements instead.

m08-array-tree.caption: **One array, two views.** The heap is stored flat (bottom), but you reason about it as a tree (top): node `i` has children at `2i+1`, `2i+2`. This min-heap only promises *parent ≤ children* — much weaker than sorted order, which is why pushes and pops stay $O(\log n)$.

m08-topk-evict.caption: **The size-K min-heap bouncer** (K = 3, tracking the 3 largest). New element 8 challenges `top()` = 5, the weakest current champion. 8 wins, 5 is evicted, and the heap becomes {7, 8, 9} — still exactly the K largest seen so far.

m08-kway.caption: **K-way merge, one step.** The heap holds only the current head of each sorted list — here {1, 4, 6}. Pop the minimum (1) onto the output, then push its successor (7) from the *same* list. The heap never grows past k.

class='t-sm t-muted'>after popping 1 from L0, push its successor 7 — heap size stays ≤ k = 3</text></svg>

m08-two-heaps.caption: **Two heaps facing each other.** `lo` (max-heap) holds the smaller half {2, 3, 5}; `hi` (min-heap) holds the larger half {7, 9}. With 5 total elements, `lo` keeps the extra one, so the median is simply `lo.top()` = 5 — read straight off the boundary.
m08-two-heaps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 660 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Two heaps meeting at the median'><text x='20' y='24' class='t-bold'>two heaps meet at the median</text><rect x='30' y='56' width='220' height='124' rx='8' class='f-panel2 s-line'/><text x='140' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>lo — MAX-heap (smaller half)</text><rect x='58' y='98' width='44' height='44' class='f-panel s-line'/><text x='80' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono'>2</text><rect x='104' y='98' width='44' height='44' class='f-panel s-line'/><text x='126' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono'>3</text><rect x='150' y='98' width='44' height='44' class='f-accent-soft s-accent'/><text x='172' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono t-accent'>5</text><text x='172' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-accent'>top = 5</text><rect x='410' y='56' width='220' height='124' rx='8' class='f-panel2 s-line'/><text x='520' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>hi — MIN-heap (larger half)</text><rect x='460' y='98' width='44' height='44' class='f-ok-soft s-ok'/><text x='482' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono t-ok'>7</text><rect x='506' y='98' width='44' height='44' class='f-panel s-line'/><text x='528' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono'>9</text><text x='482' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-ok'>top = 7</text><text x='330' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>count = 5 (odd)</text><text x='330' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='t-bold'>median = 5</text><text x='330' y='216' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>invariants: max(lo) ≤ min(hi) and |lo| − |hi| ∈ {0, 1}</text></svg>

m08-task-windows.caption: **Task Scheduler on `AAABBC`, `n = 2`.** Time is processed in windows of `n+1 = 3` slots. Full windows charge all 3 slots even when one is a forced idle (slot 5); the last window charges only the real tasks placed — total time 7.
m08-task-windows.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 660 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Task scheduler cooldown windows'><text x='20' y='24' class='t-bold'>AAABBC, n = 2 ⇒ windows of n+1 = 3</text><rect x='20' y='52' width='44' height='44' class='f-accent-soft s-accent'/><text x='42' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono t-accent'>A</text><rect x='66' y='52' width='44' s-bad'/><text x='272' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono t-bad'>−</text><rect x='296' y='52' width='44' height='44' class='f-accent-soft s-accent'/><text x='318' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='t-mono t-accent'>A</text><text x='42' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>0</text><text x='88' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='t-sm t-muted'>1</text><text x='134' y='114'
